summary:
The St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre (August 24, 1572 AD) was a targeted mass killing of French Huguenots (Calvinist Protestants) in Paris and across France, ordered by King Charles IX at the instigation of his mother Catherine de' Medici and the Catholic Guise faction, killing an estimated 5,000–30,000 people in the bloodiest single episode of the French Wars of Religion (1562–1598). The massacre began in Paris on the night of August 23–24 (the feast of St. Bartholomew), initially targeting Huguenot leaders who had gathered in Paris for the wedding of the Protestant Henry of Navarre (the future Henry IV) to the Catholic Margaret of Valois — a marriage intended to ease religious tensions. Admiral Gaspard de Coligny, the Huguenot political leader, was the first target; he survived an assassination attempt on August 22 and was murdered in his bed on the night of the massacre.

Once Parisian violence began, it spread beyond the intended political assassinations into a popular mob massacre that the royal government had neither planned nor could control: Catholic Parisian mobs attacked Huguenot neighborhoods, killing men, women, and children, throwing bodies into the Seine. The violence spread to provincial cities — Rouen, Lyon, Toulouse, Bordeaux — over the following weeks. The massacre's political consequences were profound: Henry of Navarre converted to Catholicism to survive (temporarily), Pope Gregory XIII celebrated with a Te Deum and commemorative medal, Philip II of Spain laughed for reportedly the only time in his life, and Protestant Europe was appalled. The massacre intensified the [[event:102|religious wars]] and radicalized both sides; it drove the development of resistance theories (monarchomach doctrine) arguing that subjects could resist tyrannical kings — an intellectual precursor to modern theories of popular sovereignty.
